image by = barrierbreakAccessibility Bookmarklets Every Tester Should Know
Sometimes you don’t want to fire up a full testing suite — you just want to check one thing on one page, fast. That’s exactly what accessibility bookmarklets are for. A bookmarklet is a tiny piece of JavaScript saved as a browser bookmark; click it, and it runs a check on whatever page you’re viewing. No installation, no account, no setup. For testers, developers, and designers, they’re among the quickest ways to catch accessibility issues in the moment.
Here’s a rundown of the bookmarklets worth keeping on your bookmarks bar — and how to use them well.
What Is an Accessibility Bookmarklet?
A bookmarklet is a bookmark that contains code instead of a web address. When you click it, it executes that code against the current page — highlighting headings, exposing ARIA attributes, revealing focus order, or flagging missing alt text.
Their appeal is speed and simplicity:

• Zero installation. Drag a link to your bookmarks bar and it’s ready.

• Works anywhere. Any page, any site, in your normal browser.

• Instant, visual feedback. Most overlay information directly on the page.

• Free. Nearly all of the well-known ones cost nothing.
They’re not a replacement for full audits — but as a first-look tool, they’re hard to beat.
The Bookmarklets Worth Knowing
Heading structure viewers
Screen reader users navigate largely by headings, so a broken heading hierarchy is a real barrier. A heading map bookmarklet visualizes every heading (h1–h6) on the page and its level, instantly revealing skipped levels, multiple h1s, or text that merely looks like a heading without being one.
Use it to: confirm a logical, properly nested heading outline.
Landmark and structure highlighters
These reveal a page’s ARIA landmarks and structural regions — banner, navigation, main, complementary, contentinfo — so you can see whether the page is properly divided for assistive technology.
Use it to: check that key regions are labeled and navigable.
Focus order and tab visualizers
Keyboard users move through a page in a specific order. A focus-order bookmarklet draws the tab sequence visually, exposing places where focus jumps around illogically or gets trapped.
Use it to: verify keyboard navigation follows a sensible path.
Alt text and image revealers
These display the alt text (or absence of it) for every image on the page, so you can spot missing descriptions or unhelpful ones like image01.jpg.
Use it to: quickly audit image text alternatives.
ARIA and role inspectors
These surface ARIA roles, states, and properties, helping you catch misused or missing attributes on custom components.
Use it to: sanity-check ARIA on widgets, modals, and menus.
Well-Known Free Options
A few widely used, freely available tools work as bookmarklets or in a similar one-click spirit:

• The WAVE bookmarklet / extension (WebAIM) — overlays errors and structural icons directly on the page.

• tota11y (originally from Khan Academy) — a visualization toolkit that annotates headings, contrast, labels, and more.

• ANDI (US Social Security Administration) — a detailed inspection bookmarklet widely used in government testing.

• a11y.css — a bookmarklet that flags risky or non-conforming markup with visual warnings.
Consultancies and tool makers often publish their own curated bookmarklet sets too. BarrierBreak’s guide to accessibility bookmarklets for testing is a useful companion resource for building out your collection.
How to Use Bookmarklets Effectively
Bookmarklets are powerful precisely because they’re quick — but quick can become sloppy. A few tips:

• Use them as spot-checks, not audits. They answer specific questions (“is the heading structure sound?”), not “is this page compliant?”

• Combine several. No single bookmarklet covers everything; a small kit — headings, landmarks, focus order, alt text — covers a lot of ground fast.

• Pair with keyboard and screen reader testing. Bookmarklets show you structure; only actual assistive-technology use confirms the experience.

• Verify the tool is current. Some community bookmarklets fall out of maintenance; confirm yours still behaves as expected.

• Learn to read the output. A highlighted landmark or heading map only helps if you know what “good” looks like.
Common Mistakes to Avoid

• Treating a clean bookmarklet check as compliance. They’re a first look, not a verdict.

• Relying on just one. Each reveals a slice; use a few together.

• Skipping manual testing. Bookmarklets can’t tell you if someone can complete a task with a screen reader.

• Using unmaintained tools and trusting stale output.
For confidence beyond quick checks, many teams pair bookmarklets with expert evaluation; the accessibility specialists at BarrierBreak combine automated, manual, and assistive-technology testing because no single quick tool tells the whole story.
Conclusion
Accessibility bookmarklets are the Swiss Army knife of quick accessibility testing — one-click tools that reveal headings, landmarks, focus order, alt text, and ARIA on any page, instantly and for free. Keep a small, well-chosen set on your bookmarks bar and you’ll catch a surprising number of issues in seconds, right as you build or review. Just remember what they are: a fast first look, not a full audit. Use them to spot problems early, then follow up with keyboard and screen reader testing to confirm what only real assistive-technology use can. Together, that’s a fast, practical path to more accessible pages.
FAQ
What is an accessibility bookmarklet? It’s a browser bookmark containing JavaScript instead of a web address. Clicking it runs an accessibility check on the current page — such as visualizing headings, landmarks, focus order, or alt text — with no installation required.
Are accessibility bookmarklets free? Nearly all the well-known ones are free. Tools like the WAVE bookmarklet, tota11y, ANDI, and a11y.css cost nothing to use.
How do I install a bookmarklet? Usually you drag a provided link to your browser’s bookmarks bar, or create a bookmark and paste in the code. Once saved, clicking it runs the check on whatever page you’re viewing.
Can bookmarklets replace a full accessibility audit? No. They’re excellent for fast spot-checks of specific things, but they don’t cover everything and can’t confirm real usability. A full audit combines automated tools, manual testing, and screen reader testing.
Which bookmarklets should I start with? A practical starter kit covers heading structure, landmarks/regions, focus order, and alt text. Add ARIA inspection as you get comfortable. Between them, you can quickly check the most common structural issues.
Do bookmarklets work on any browser? Most work across major browsers since they’re just JavaScript in a bookmark. A few tools also offer extension versions with extra features, but the bookmarklet form is the most portable.
